source.definition: To direct your eyes towards someone or something in order to see or examine it carefully.

explanation.sourceText: The English phrasal verb 'look at' combines 'look', from Old English 'lōcian' meaning 'to gaze or glance', with the preposition 'at' indicating direction or focus.
explanation.targetText: Verbul compus englezesc „look at” combină „look”, din engleza veche „lōcian” care însemna „a privi sau a arunca o privire”, cu prepoziția „at” care indică direcția sau focalizarea.

target.definition: A se uita cu atenție la cineva sau ceva.

explanation.sourceText: The Romanian verb 'a privi' derives from the Latin 'privare', which originally meant 'to deprive', but in Romanian evolved semantically to mean 'to see, to look'.
explanation.targetText: Verbul românesc „a privi” provine din latină, din 'privare' care inițial însemna 'a priva', dar în română a evoluat semantic spre 'a vedea, a privi'.

examples.sourceText: I look at the picture carefully.
She looks out of the window.
Look at me when I speak to you!

learningTips.text: Remember 'a privi' sounds like 'private' — imagine you are privately focusing your eyes carefully.
The English phrase 'look at' helps you remember 'a privi' because both involve directing your gaze intentionally.
Think of 'a privi' as 'preview' without the 'ew' — preview means to look ahead or examine.
Since 'a privi' comes from Latin 'privare' meaning 'to deprive', imagine you 'deprive' your eyes of distractions to focus and look carefully.
